sessions.details: Recorded 1970-05-26 for Mike Harding's show in the Sounds Of The Seventies strand. Available on expanded edition of Atomic Roooster (Castle Music).
Recorded 1971-03-22 for Harding. Only one track TX here.  Available on reissued CD of Death Walks Behind You (Castle Music).
Recorded 1972-08-10 for Pete Drummond show. Only one track TX here. Available on reissued CD of Made In England (Castle Music).

track_listing.details: LP-The Nature Of The Beast; Capitol

track_listing.track: 'Time To Get Out (LP-Defector)' (Charisma) Wrongly announced as 'Slogans,' which is side A track 3.

track_listing.details: LP-The Wild, The Willing And The Innocent; Chrysalis
LP-The Nature Of The Beast; Capitol

transcript.text: ...opened only five days ago. That's the news on Radio 1 at 2 minutes past 10.
On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Good evening and welcome once again to the program that we call The Friday Rock Show.
Thomas the Vance here, and tonight you'll hear four tracks that you'll find heavily featured on Saxon's latest LP.
And out of the archives, some excellent old sessions from 71, 72, and 70 by Atomic Rooster.
Also, we've got some classic cuts by Pat Travers, Steve Hackett, Crosby, Stills, and Nash, and Stillwater.
Among others, some new stuff by UFO, by Styx, by Gillen, by Trevor Raven, by Steve Winwood.
Not forgetting our Friday night connection, the answer to last week's and the new one.
But this evening, we start with this.
While the darkness can help you hide
Pat Travers had a track from an album called Makin' Magic.
That track was called Rock and Roll Susie.
For all the people who attend a certain place over there near Dublin,
a place that is called Kellett's Writing School.
Or possibly it's Kellett's Writing School.
I can't really read the writing here.
Well, it's probably my fault, not theirs.
Also including the three musketeers and Jillian and Angie and Rachel and Rosie.
Best wishes and God bless from, I think it's Greg.
And before that, you heard something from the brand new album by April Wine,
the album that has the title The Nature of the Beast.
The track we played was Sign of the Gypsy Queen.
And April Wine, of course, are going to be touring starting next week,
and backing them is going to be Diamond Head, which is nice news.
And also, April Wine, at least a couple of them,
are going to be appearing on The Rock on Saturday,
and they said the name of the wrong program.
I just want to put something to rights here,
and also to please a gentleman by the name of Richard Power,
who lives in number 81, the Assores, Heath Charnock, Chorley in Lanx.
Richard, you're absolutely right.
The Crocus track that I played the other day,
the studio version of She's Got Everything,
actually was one of the songs that they performed at the Reading Festival,
but I'd totally forgotten that,
so you and 18 people were absolutely right.
We were wrong.
Not the first time that we've been wrong, and probably not the last, but we try.
It's 10.13. Here's the first of four by Saxon.
excellent track from an excellent album that came out some time ago that of course was steve hackett
from the album defector the track was entitled slogans 21 minutes up to 10 on the friday rock
show that's for all the freaks at catterton i think it is near oxford especially stewart turbo
jet turkey i think possibly his name is and neil i can't see how but and tim who wrote the letter
who wants me to say hello to everybody at wolverston's hall near f switch steve hackett
also was requested by Jean and Hans
in Sue and Jenny and Hugh and Barbara
and Mel, and most particularly for
Graham Henderson, who attends
or probably did attend
a sort of academic
place until about Christmas. I think he gave up
at Christmas. Anyway, he lives
these days at 57 Woodlands Terrace
in Dundee. And the Steve Hackett
track also should be dedicated to M. Parker.
He lives in Bradshaw in Bolton in
Lancs, who has a PPS on his little letter here.
He says, can I please have Tony Wilson's
autograph. I'm terribly sorry, M. Parker, but Mr. Wilson can't write. I can say that
because he's not here. Oh, the track you heard before, that was by Saxon. It was a track
called Heavy Metal Thunder. We've got three more by them. I'm going to play you something
now you'll know, but there is a difference about it. See if you can spot it.
A UFO from their brand new album called The Wild, The Willing and The Innocent. The track
you heard was called long gone which of course they haven't been really they hit the road next
tuesday incidentally on a brand new tour now i've been asked to tell you about that album by a
gentleman called duncan who lives at number three bain square stornoway in the isle of lewis who
wants me to mention ian and callum and neil and hugh and shuggy and lodge and all the other heavy
metal freaks in stornoway well what i can tell you about that album duncan is well basically what
i've said and also the fact that it's released next friday on chrysalis so now you know and
Upon that you heard Crosby, Stills & Nash from an album that's just come out
that has the title Crosby, Stills & Nash Replay.
The track I played was first recorded in 1970.
The track called Carry On.
Slightly different, though, because in October 1980,
Stephen Stills added Electric League Guitar and Billy Meeker added a drum track.
Did you notice the difference?
I must confess that if I hadn't read it on the back of this particular compilation,
I don't think I would have.
28 and a half minutes before 11.
Here's our archive session.
The first of two sets by Atomic Rooster.
A little trio when they were put there together in May 1970.
That was Atomic Rooster out of our session archives.
And a couple of tracks, Seven Lonely Streets and Save Me.
And at that particular time of recording, it was Vincent Crane on the keyboards,
Johnny Mandela on the guitar, and Rick Parnell on the drums.
You're listening to the Friday Rock Show.
It's 18 minutes to 11.
And here's something brand new by Styx.
Those southern boys boogie, they really boogie.
That was recorded way back in 1977.
It's by a band called Stillwater, from an album of the same name.
Its title was Sam's Jam.
And I'm happy to play that again on the radio.
Haven't played it for years, in fact, since it first came out in 1977.
It was also featured, incidentally, but without the vocal,
on that review of the 70s during the Rock Week on BBC Two,
on the Old Grey Whistle Test, which I got on tape at home, on videotape.
I don't know whether it's legal or not.
I don't even care.
There are a million of these recorders around the country.
I just know that every time I get around to that particular portion
of that review of the 70s, that Stillwater track knocks me out.
And I'd rather think it would have knocked out Rob Whitehead
of Borghurst in Basingstoke, because he described it to us,
and we phoned up the old Great Whistle Test and found out what it was,
and we just played it for you.
Nine minutes and 30, a great southern boogie.
Also played for a gentleman, if I can find his name.
Well, I can't, but for some other gentleman who just writes cheers
and then did a little caricature of a motorbike
and a fella who wanted to hear some, well, long southern boogie.
The track before that is something from the new album by Styx.
The album's called Paradise Theatre.
The track we played was Snowblind.
And it was for Brian Thompson of 38 Selby Drive in Inverurie.
Two minutes to 11.
Here's the new single, which is by Gillan,
on first hearing possibly not too commercial,
but on second hearing it grows.
Have a listen to it with those words in mind.
It's called Mutually Assured Destruction.
For Saxon and 20,000 Feet.
Five minutes after 11, before that you heard the brand new single by Gillen,
which comes in a very, very beautifully, what would you call it,
sort of gatefold 45 sleeve with a one-act play that's been written by Gillen in it,
all about the nuclear peril.
Excuse me.
I think it's a really good single.
Its title is Mutually Assured Destruction.
Right, it's time for the Friday Night Connection.
The tracks we played you last week were
Wake Up and Make Love to Me, Ian Dury,
from the album New Boots and Panties.
And we played a track called Full For Your Stockings.
That was by ZZ Chop.
And then we played Carousel Umbra by Led Zeppelin.
Got a letter in front of me from Miss VJ Stockley
of 49 Gladstone Road, Port Slade in Brighton.
Laval, along with all the other ladies in the world,
wears panties stockings and carousel and bra got it panties stockings and bra was the answer
obviously val you recognized your own apparel in there congratulations you get yourself a
10 pound record token we were going to send you some clothing coupons but we thought otherwise
miss vj stockley has put together her own friday night connection as well
and it starts off like this.
My Connections were together by Val Stockley
of Port Slade in Brighton.
Tasty one, Val?
It's down to you.
If you think you know what the answer is,
all you have to do is to jot it down
on any form of indication you like,
which I am perpetually saying on this show,
but I mean it.
Letter, postcard, telegram,
whatever you like to use.
And send it to The Friday Night Connection,
The Friday Rock Show,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4, WWF.
Friday Night Connection, BBC Radio 1,
broadcasting house if you want to be formal,
London, W1A, 4WW.
Oh, but don't forget to put together your own Friday Night Connection as well,
and you might get yourself a £10 record token.
Here's Saxon.
Really talented guy, that guy.
His name is Trevor Rabin.
That's a track from his new album, the album called Wolf.
The track you heard was called Heard You Cry Wolf.
And just as a sort of indication of the respect that that fella has,
Playing on that track also are people like Simon Phillips and Jack Bruce,
and Manfred Mann and Rabbit, Chris Thompson, Stevie Langer and Noel McCalla,
ex of that ace man called Moon are doing the backup vocals.
There's a guy that one day is going to find himself a niche and be a star,
South African fella Trevor Raven from the album Wolf.
I'd like to dedicate that to a lady by the name of Linda Black,
who lives up there, and I think it's Dunblane,
and she wants me to mention Christine Arthur, who's listening in at the moment,
and also Jillian and Jane, whose birthdays are on the 6th and the 8th of January, respectively, or rather were.
Oh, Alan Knowles, who lives in Etherston Green in Streatham, says here, mentioned Dick and Sharon,
and can I please wish Millwall the best of luck, as they needed it, says here, in brackets,
on Saturday when they play Fulham, and I do.
28 Minutes to Midnight, out of the archives from the early 70s, Atomic Rooster.
Do you notice the change in the vocalists?
That one was recorded in 1972, featured the voice there of Mr. Chris Farlow.
The band, of course, were Atomic Rooster.
The track was called Stand By Me.
Before that, Atomic Rooster again, but that time recorded in 1971,
and that track was called Tomorrow Night.
It was, of course, the studio version of their hit single.
I'd like to dedicate this track to a lady who's been over here for three years.
She's been living in Suffolk.
She's American.
She goes home shortly.
Her name is Rachel Robertson.
She wants me to say hello to a guy by the name of Andy down in Plymouth.
now got time to take the night train didn't you this is steve winwood that's april wine from the
new album called the nature of the beast the track was entitled caught in the crossfire
and before that something from ark of a diver the solo album and when he he says solo he means so
because he plays everything on the blooming record steve winwood and a track called night
train it's a good album try to get hold of it if you can afford it kath hill was 16 on the 6th of
january happy birthday for that particular day kath she wants me to play cheated by praying
mantis especially for all the people she met in the balcony at bristol's coulston hall at the
triumph gig she was the welsh girl in the ufo t-shirt i'm a saxon a final one from or rather
from them tonight it was called sixth form girls and while i was playing i suddenly thought about
all the people who might be in dormitories under the blankets like what i used to be years and
years ago when i was in school just you're all safe and warm and you've enjoyed the music
If there's ever anything you'd like to hear on this program,
all you have to do is to jot it down on a postcard letter,
anything you'd like, and send it to me.
My name is Tommy Vance.
The program is called The Friday Rock Show,
and the address goes like this.
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A 4WW.
And you can bet your bottom dollar that every letter gets good attention,
although not a speedy answer if there's a query in it,
because I'm not that good at writing back,
but eventually, believe me, I do.
And that's it for this edition of The Friday Rock Show.
Many thanks to your company.
I trust you have enjoyed the program.
Thanks to my engineers tonight.
Also, a quick reminder that on Rock on Saturday tomorrow,
you can hear Chevy, April Wine, Bernard Shainer, and some other good music.
Following the midnight beep-beep-beeps,
the 275-285 transmitters are going to be closing down.
Tony Wilson produces this program. He's not here tonight. He's in Birmingham.
But he wants me specifically to thank you very much for the birthday cards.
Isn't it great to be 50-tone?
And that's only his ankle measurement.
This is Thomas Fence in London
Wherever you happen to be at this very moment of time
I trust you're a happy person
Do take care of yourselves, I'll see you tomorrow
Till then, goodnight and God bless
It's midnight and we welcome VHF listeners
who've now joined us on Radio 2
This is Patrick Lunt in the Midnight Newsroom
